content.text: A Shared Project is a persistent collaborative workspace inside Claude's Team or Enterprise plan where multiple team members share the same custom instruction, knowledge files, conversation history, and artifacts. It solves a problem that individual Claude subscriptions create: when everyone works in private sessions, knowledge is fragmented, teammates duplicate work, and there is no shared memory of what the team has already produced.

Unlike a conversation — which starts fresh and belongs to one person — a Shared Project is a named, configured environment that persists indefinitely and is visible to everyone who has been added to it.

attrs.alt: Shared Project anatomy showing custom instruction, knowledge files, member roles, conversation history, and artifacts

content.text: The diagram above shows the four components that make up a Shared Project: the custom instruction applied to every conversation, the knowledge files Claude reads on each turn, the member roster with roles, the shared conversation history visible to all, and the artifact store where reusable outputs are saved.
Core Components
Custom Instruction

The custom instruction is a text prompt that is injected before every conversation inside the Project, for every member. It functions as a persistent system prompt — Claude sees it at the start of every turn, even though members do not type it manually.

The instruction is set by the Project owner and can only be edited by owners or editors (not viewers). Changes to the instruction take effect immediately for all future conversations. Existing conversation history is not retroactively affected — conversations that have already started continue with the instruction that was active when they began.
Maximum length: approximately 2,000–4,000 tokens. Very long instructions leave less context budget for the actual conversation and knowledge files. Keep instructions focused.
Format: plain text or markdown. Markdown structure (headers, bullet points) improves Claude's interpretation of the instruction.
Scope: write instructions that describe the role, output format, constraints, and domain. Avoid including frequently-changing information in the instruction — put that in knowledge files instead.
Knowledge Files

Knowledge files are documents uploaded to the Project that Claude reads before generating each response. They represent the team's shared reference library — the information Claude should always have access to within this Project, without members having to paste it into every conversation.
Supported file types: PDF, DOCX, TXT, Markdown, CSV, XLSX, code files. Claude extracts text from all formats; complex formatting in PDFs may not parse cleanly.
File count limit: up to 20 files per Project.
File size: typically up to 10 MB per file, 200 MB total per Project.
Upload management: owners and editors can add or remove files. Viewers cannot upload.
Version control: knowledge files are not versioned inside the Project. When you upload a new version of a file, the old file should be removed first to avoid Claude reading contradictory information.

Context window note: every knowledge file adds to the context Claude must process on each turn. If the Project has 20 large files, the available context for the actual conversation is reduced. Prioritise the 5–8 files members actually reference most, and archive older ones outside the Project.
Member Roles
Projects support three roles that control what each member can do:
Role
Can read conversations
Can start conversations
Can edit instruction/files
Can add/remove members
Owner
Yes
Yes
Yes
Yes
Editor
Yes
Yes
Yes
No
Viewer
Yes
Yes — within Project
No
No

All three roles can start conversations inside the Project and see all conversations that others have had. The distinction is whether they can modify the Project configuration (instruction, files, members). A typical configuration makes the team lead the Owner, senior team members Editors, and the rest of the team Viewers.
Shared Conversation History

Every conversation started inside a Shared Project is visible to all Project members. This is the feature that most distinguishes Shared Projects from individual use: members can pick up where a colleague left off, review how a problem was solved previously, and build on outputs without re-asking the same questions.
Conversations are labelled with the author's name and the time they were created.
Any member can continue a conversation started by another member — the conversation remains attributed to the original author but any member can add turns.
Conversations are not deleted when the member who created them leaves the workspace. The content persists and remains visible to remaining members.
There is no branching or locking — if two members add turns to the same conversation simultaneously, their messages interleave. Avoid concurrent editing of the same conversation.
Artifacts

Artifacts are discrete outputs — code files, documents, tables, analysis results — that are saved explicitly within the Project rather than being buried in conversation history. When Claude generates a substantial output (a Python script, a report draft, a data table), a member can save it as an Artifact to make it easy for the team to find and reuse.
Supported artifact types: code (any language), Markdown documents, SVG images, HTML pages, CSV data.
Artifacts are editable: any Editor or Owner can modify a saved Artifact. Versions are not tracked — the last save overwrites.
Artifacts persist independently of the conversation that produced them. The conversation can be deleted without losing the Artifact.
Members can reference an Artifact in a new conversation by opening it from the Project sidebar.
Personal vs Shared Projects

attrs.alt: Side-by-side comparison of Personal Project and Shared Project capabilities

content.text: The most important distinction from the comparison above: admin users cannot read conversation content in Shared Projects. Admins can see that a Project exists and who is in it, but cannot open individual conversations. This preserves member privacy even within a team context.
Creating and Configuring a Shared Project
Step-by-Step Creation
Open claude.ai and navigate to the Projects sidebar. Click New Project.

Enter a name that clearly identifies the team and use case: 'Engineering — Code Review', 'Marketing — Campaign Copy', 'Legal — Contract Drafting'. Vague names ('AI Workspace', 'Team Project') cause confusion as the Project list grows.
Open Project Settings (gear icon). In the Custom Instruction field, paste the instruction for this Project. Save.
In the Knowledge Files section, upload reference files the team needs. Add a brief filename that describes the content: 'refund-policy-v3.pdf' not 'document.pdf'.
Click Share Project. Add team members by their workspace email addresses. Assign Owner or Editor to those who should manage the Project; assign Viewer to everyone else.
Test: start a conversation, type a question that references the knowledge files, and verify that Claude applies the instruction and retrieves information from the files.
Writing a Useful Custom Instruction

The custom instruction is the single highest-leverage configuration in a Shared Project. A poorly written instruction causes inconsistent behaviour that erodes team trust in the Project. A well-written instruction creates a reliable, focused assistant.

# Good instruction structure (example: Customer Support Project)

## Role
You are a Tier 1 support agent for Acme Inc. Your job is to help customers
resolve issues quickly and accurately using the knowledge files in this Project.

## Behaviour rules
- Always check the product-faq.md file before generating an answer from memory.
- Do not promise refunds, credits, or escalations without checking the
  refund-policy.pdf file.
- If the answer is not in the knowledge files, say so explicitly and ask the
  customer for more detail rather than guessing.

## Output format
- Use plain text, not markdown, in responses (customers read on mobile).
- Keep responses under 200 words unless the customer explicitly asks for detail.
- End every response with a one-line next step: "Next step: ..."

## Escalation
- Billing disputes → transfer to billing@acme.com
- Technical defects → open a ticket at support.acme.com with tag #tier2
Managing Knowledge File Updates

Knowledge files require active maintenance. A file uploaded six months ago may be outdated, and Claude will confidently serve stale information from it. Establish a cadence for reviewing Project knowledge files.
File type
Review cadence
Update trigger
Policy documents
Monthly
Any policy change
Product specifications
On each release
Feature launch or deprecation
Contact directories
Quarterly
Org structure changes
Legal / regulatory refs
On regulatory change
New legislation, court decisions
Style guides
Annually
Brand refresh or writing standard change
Operational Patterns
One Project per Workflow, Not per Team

Teams that are new to Shared Projects often create one Project for the whole team. This leads to a cluttered instruction ('you are a support agent AND a code reviewer AND a report writer') and a conversation history that is hard to navigate. Create separate Projects for distinct workflows:
'Customer Support — Tier 1' with support agent instruction and FAQ/policy files.
'Engineering — Python Review' with code review instruction and coding standards.
'Marketing — Campaign Briefs' with brand voice instruction and campaign templates.
Using Projects as Onboarding Tools

A well-maintained Shared Project is a practical onboarding tool for new team members. When a new hire joins and is added to the relevant Projects, they immediately have access to the team's established workflows, reference documents, and conversation history showing how colleagues handle common situations. The instruction acts as a written process guide; the conversation history shows real examples.
Archiving Stale Projects

Over time, Projects accumulate that are no longer actively used. Stale Projects clutter the sidebar and may contain outdated knowledge files. The Project owner can archive a Project (or delete it permanently). Archive is preferable for Projects that may need to be referenced later; deletion is permanent and removes all conversations and artifacts.
Context Window Budget in Shared Projects

Every conversation in a Shared Project consumes context window budget from three sources: the custom instruction, the knowledge files, and the actual conversation messages. Understanding this budget is important for designing Projects that remain effective as they scale.
How Context Is Allocated

Claude's maximum context window depends on the model. Claude Sonnet 4.6 and Opus 4.8 support up to 200,000 tokens of input context. Within a Shared Project conversation, that budget is consumed roughly as follows:
Component
Typical token budget
Notes
Custom instruction
200–2,000 tokens
Injected on every turn; keep as concise as purpose allows
Knowledge files
Each file adds proportionally; 20 large files can consume most of the window
Conversation history
Grows with each turn
Long conversations gradually consume more of the remaining budget
Current turn (user + response)
The actual user message and Claude's response

When the conversation history plus instruction plus knowledge files approaches the context limit, Claude begins to 'forget' the earliest parts of the conversation. This is not an error — it is how transformer-based models handle context overflow. For long-running Projects, members should start fresh conversations rather than adding indefinitely to a single conversation thread.
Knowledge File Size Strategy

The most common cause of degraded Project performance is uploading too much to knowledge files. A 200-page PDF consumes thousands of tokens on every single turn, reducing the space available for conversation content. The right strategy depends on how Claude uses the file:
High-frequency reference:
Files Claude needs to consult on almost every turn (e.g. a 2-page FAQ, a short policy summary). These justify the constant context cost. Keep them small — under 5 KB if possible.
Low-frequency detail:

Files that are only needed for specific edge cases (e.g. a detailed regulatory reference, a 200-page product spec). Do not upload these as knowledge files. Instead, have members paste the relevant excerpt into the conversation when needed.
Summaries over originals:

For long source documents, create a summarised version specifically for the Project. A 500-word summary of a 50-page policy document often serves Claude better than the original — the summary is denser in information per token.
Advanced Custom Instruction Patterns
Instruction Inheritance for Related Projects

When an organisation has many related Projects — several customer support Projects for different product lines, or multiple engineering review Projects — a common antipattern is copying and slightly modifying the same base instruction for each. This creates maintenance debt: when the base instruction needs updating, all copies must be updated manually.

The recommended pattern is to factor shared policy into a base instruction template maintained outside Claude, and then generate Project-specific instructions from that template when setting up each Project. A simple Python script can maintain this:

import re

BASE_INSTRUCTION = """
## Role
You are a {role} for {team_name}. Follow the {policy_doc} for all decisions.

## Behaviour rules
- Always check knowledge files before generating from memory.
- Do not make commitments outside the scope of {scope}.
- Escalate to {escalation_contact} if the issue requires approval.

## Output format
- Use {output_format}.
- Keep responses under {max_words} words unless the member asks for detail.
"""

def make_instruction(role, team_name, policy_doc, scope, escalation_contact,
                     output_format, max_words):
    return BASE_INSTRUCTION.format(
        role=role,
        team_name=team_name,
        policy_doc=policy_doc,
        scope=scope,
        escalation_contact=escalation_contact,
        output_format=output_format,
        max_words=max_words,
    )

# Usage:
support_instr = make_instruction(
    role="Tier 1 support agent",
    team_name="Acme Customer Support",
    policy_doc="refund-policy-v3.pdf",
    scope="Tier 1 issues (general questions, order status)",
    escalation_contact="billing@acme.com",
    output_format="plain text, no markdown",
    max_words=200,
)
print(support_instr)  # paste into Project custom instruction
Dynamic Content in Instructions

Instructions are static text — they cannot reference the current date or call external APIs. But a useful workaround for time-sensitive Projects (where the instruction should reference a current price list, for example) is to maintain a short 'pinned' knowledge file that contains frequently-updated dynamic data, and instruct Claude to always check it:

## Instruction excerpt for a pricing Project

Before answering any question about pricing, check the file
`current-prices-LIVE.csv`. This file is updated daily. Never quote prices
from memory — always read the file.

If you cannot find a price in the file, say "I don't have a current price
for that — please check pricing.acme.com or contact sales@acme.com.""
The file `current-prices-LIVE.csv` is then the one file the team updates regularly (re-uploading a new version). All other knowledge files are stable references.
Access Control and Privacy
What Admins Can See

Organisation and workspace admins can see the list of Projects, the Project name, its members, and metadata (creation date, owner, file count). They cannot open conversations inside a Project or read message content. This is by design — it ensures that sensitive team discussions inside a Project remain private to Project members, not visible to IT admins.

This has a practical implication: if a Project contains problematic content (e.g. a member has used it in a way that violates acceptable-use policy), an admin must rely on the Project owner to investigate the contents. There is no admin override to read conversation content on the Team or Enterprise tiers through the UI.
Removing a Member from a Project

When a member is removed from a Project (not the workspace — just the Project), they immediately lose access to the Project sidebar, conversations, and artifacts. Conversations they created remain in the Project and are visible to remaining members. This behaviour is important for continuity: the team does not lose access to prior work when someone leaves a Project.
Sharing a Project with the Whole Workspace

Projects can be configured to share with all workspace members automatically. When this setting is enabled, anyone who joins the workspace (now or in the future) is automatically added to the Project. This is appropriate for organisation-wide Projects (e.g. 'Company Handbook Q&A') but should be used carefully for Projects with sensitive content, since all new members inherit access.
Troubleshooting Shared Projects
Claude is not following the custom instruction

Verify that the member is starting conversations from inside the Project (clicking New Chat while in the Project), not from the global sidebar. Conversations started outside the Project do not inherit the Project's instruction. Also confirm that the instruction was saved successfully — reload the Project settings and verify the instruction text is still present.
Claude is not referencing a knowledge file

Check the file count — Projects support a maximum of 20 files. If the Project is at the limit, older files may be deprioritised. Remove files that are no longer needed. Also check for format issues: PDFs with scanned images (no text layer) produce empty text on extraction. Re-upload as OCR-processed PDF or convert to DOCX or TXT.
New members cannot see the Project

Confirm the member has accepted their workspace invitation — pending invitations do not grant Project access. Also verify they have been explicitly added to the Project (or that 'share with all workspace members' is enabled). A common mistake is adding someone to the workspace but forgetting to add them to the specific Project.
Conversation history is growing too long to use effectively

Start a new conversation for new tasks rather than appending indefinitely to a long thread. For ongoing work that needs context from a previous conversation, copy the key summary from the previous conversation into the start of the new one. Alternatively, save key decisions and outputs as Artifacts so they are reachable without scrolling through conversation history.
Summary

Shared Projects give teams a persistent, configured Claude workspace with a common instruction, shared knowledge files, team-visible conversation history, and reusable artifacts. They eliminate the inefficiency of individual members configuring the same context repeatedly and make team AI usage discoverable and consistent. Effective use requires deliberate instruction writing, active knowledge file maintenance, and a one-Project-per-workflow organisation pattern.
